items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: What is the key difference between mitosis and meiosis?
items.correct: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells; meiosis produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes).
items.misconception: Mitosis and meiosis both produce gametes; they are basically the same.
items.random_wrong: Mitosis happens in plants and meiosis happens in animals.
items.why: The similar names lead to confusion; students mix up which process produces gametes vs body cells.

items.q: In osmosis, does water move from where solute is concentrated to where it is dilute?
items.correct: No — water moves from a dilute solution (high water potential) to a concentrated one (low water potential).
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Sugar chemically attracts and pulls water molecules towards it.
items.why: Students apply 'high to low' to the solute instead of the water; osmosis is about water moving down its own concentration (potential) gradient.

items.q: Does diffusion require the cell to use energy?
items.correct: No — diffusion is passive and needs no energy; only active transport against a gradient requires energy.
items.misconception: Cells use energy to make substances diffuse.
items.random_wrong: Diffusion only happens in living cells that respire.
items.why: Students assume all cell movement costs energy, but diffusion results from random particle motion down a gradient and happens even in non-living systems.

items.q: Which of these is a prokaryote?
items.why: Correct answer: Bacterium. “Human skin cell” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's aerobic respiration. “protein synthesis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A bacterium is 2 µm long. How many fit end-to-end across a 1 mm gap?
items.why: Exact answer: 500 bacteria. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
